concerns.text: The central claim regarding 'Attribution Hallucination' relies on SAA scores (e.g., 76.0 for Gemini) but lacks statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) to prove these differences are not due to random variance. The abstract notes 'statistical analysis performed' but the Results section (Table 1, lines 330-360) only reports point estimates. Without error bars or significance tests, the 'pervasive' nature of the gap is anecdotal.

The ground-truth annotation pipeline (Section 3.3, lines 180-195) uses a single MLLM (Qwen3-VL-235B) to identify 'Crucial Evidence' via masking ablation. This introduces a circularity risk: if the ground truth is defined by the model's own failure to answer without the evidence, the metric may be biased towards that specific model's reasoning patterns. No inter-annotator agreement (human or multi-model) is reported to validate the 'Crucial Evidence' labels.

The evaluation metric SAA (Section 4.1, lines 230-240) combines Answer Correctness (LLM judge) and Evidence Localization (IoU). The LLM judge (Qwen3-VL-235B) is used to score the very models being tested. While Appendix B.2 claims 'no statistically significant deviation' from human experts (p>0.05), the sample size for this validation (N=200) is not explicitly linked to the total test set (N=1897) in the main text, and the power of the Friedman test to detect subtle judge biases is unverified.

Table 1 (lines 330-360) highlights 'best' and 'second-best' results across 20 models. The header notes 'Multiple-comparisons correction (Benjamini-Hochberg FDR)' was applied, but the text does not report the adjusted p-values or the specific q-threshold used to determine significance. Without this, the ranking claims (e.g., Gemini vs. GPT) are susceptible to false discovery.
